steps.title: Set Up and Model Rhyming
steps.instructions: Tape 8 picture cards around the room (on walls, chairs, floor). Say: 'We're going to find words that rhyme! Rhyming words sound the same at the end. Listen: CAT... HAT. Cat and hat rhyme! Let's find the hat!' Walk together to the hat card. High five!
steps.materialsInstructions: Place cards visibly around room before starting. Keep cards at Luca's eye level.

steps.title: Find Rhyming Pictures with Movement
steps.instructions: Parent says a target word. Child moves (walk, hop, crawl, or animal walk) to find the picture that rhymes. After each match, celebrate and move to the next round. Use the questions below in order.

questions.prompt: I say CAT. Can you hop like a bunny to find a picture that rhymes with cat?

options.feedback: Yes! Cat and bat rhyme! They both end with 'at'. Great listening!
Dog is an animal, but listen: cat... dog. They don't rhyme. Let's try again!

questions.explanation: Cat and bat rhyme because they both end with 'at'.
hints: Listen: cat... bat. Do they sound the same at the end?
It's an animal that flies at night. Does bat rhyme with cat?
Point toward the bat card and say: 'Is this one bat? Does bat rhyme with cat?'

questions.prompt: I say HAT. Can you walk like a penguin to find a picture that rhymes with hat?

options.feedback: Perfect! Hat and rat rhyme! You found it!
Frog is green and jumps, but listen: hat... frog. They don't rhyme. Try again!

questions.explanation: Hat and rat rhyme because they both end with 'at'.
hints: Listen: hat... rat. Do they end the same way?
It's a little animal with a long tail. Does rat rhyme with hat?
Point near the rat card and say: 'Check here. Does rat rhyme with hat?'

questions.prompt: I say DOG. Can you crawl like a bear to find a picture that rhymes with dog?

options.feedback: Yes! Dog and frog rhyme! They both say 'og' at the end. Great job!
Cat is soft and furry, but listen: dog... cat. They don't rhyme. Let's find frog!

questions.explanation: Dog and frog rhyme because they both end with 'og'.
hints: Listen: dog... frog. Do they sound the same at the end?
It's green and jumps. Does frog rhyme with dog?
Point to the frog card and say: 'Is this frog? Does frog rhyme with dog?'

questions.prompt: I say LOG. Can you march like a soldier to find a picture that rhymes with log?

options.feedback: Awesome! Log and hog rhyme! You're a rhyming superstar!
Bat flies, but listen: log... bat. They don't rhyme. Let's find hog!

questions.explanation: Log and hog rhyme because they both end with 'og'.
hints: Listen: log... hog. Do they rhyme?
It's a pink farm animal. Does hog rhyme with log?
Walk near the hog card and say: 'Is this a hog? Does hog rhyme with log?'

steps.materialsInstructions: Point to general area if child is stuck. Offer 2 choices if needed: 'Does bat rhyme with cat, or does dog rhyme with cat?'

steps.title: Celebrate and Review
steps.instructions: Give high five! Say: 'You found rhyming words! Cat and bat rhyme. Dog and frog rhyme. You moved and listened so well!' Let Luca pick his favorite rhyme pair and say it together one more time.
steps.materialsInstructions: No materials needed. Praise effort and listening.

materialsNeeded: 8 picture cards: cat, hat, bat, rat, dog, frog, log, hog
Tape or place cards around room at child height
Open space for movement
list.parentInstructions: This is a short, playful rhyme game. Luca will move his body to find pictures that rhyme. Use short sentences, model the first one together, and celebrate effort. If he struggles after 2 tries, give him a choice between 2 pictures. Keep it fun and quick—6 minutes total. End with success!

list.theme: Game-based letter recognition using Luca's name and familiar letters
list.title: Letter Bingo Adventure
list.objective: Identify and recognize uppercase letters L, U, C, A, M, B through a playful bingo game format

steps.title: Letter Body Warm-Up
steps.instructions: Before sitting down, spend 30 seconds making letter shapes with your bodies. Say: 'Let's make the letter L with our arms!' (straight arm down, arm out to side). 'Now make the letter A!' (arms up like a roof). Make it silly and fun. Then say: 'Now you're ready for Letter Bingo!'
steps.materialsInstructions: No materials needed—just your bodies and some space to move.

steps.title: Letter Bingo Game - Round 1
steps.instructions: Place the bingo board in front of Luca. Point to each letter and say its name clearly: 'This is L. This is U. This is C. This is A. This is M. This is B.' Give Luca the 6 cover objects. Say: 'I'm going to call out a letter. You find it and cover it!' Call letters in random order. Use short, clear directions: 'Find the letter M.' 'Cover the A.' When he covers all 6, celebrate: 'BINGO! You did it!'

questions.prompt: Find the letter L

options.feedback: Yes! That's L, the first letter in LUCA!

questions.explanation: The letter L is shaped like a corner. It has one line going down and one line going across at the bottom.
hints: It's the first letter in your name, Luca!
It looks like a corner or an upside-down chair.
Point to it: This one right here!

questions.prompt: Find the letter U

options.feedback: Great! That's U!

questions.explanation: The letter U looks like a cup or a smile. It curves at the bottom.
hints: It's the second letter in your name!
It looks like a cup or a big smile.
Point to it: Right here!

questions.prompt: Find the letter C

options.feedback: Perfect! That's the letter C!

questions.explanation: The letter C is curved and looks like it's open on one side, like a moon or a mouth.
hints: It's the third letter in LUCA!
It looks like a moon or an open mouth.
Let's find it together: here it is!

questions.prompt: Find the letter A

options.feedback: Awesome! That's A!

questions.explanation: The letter A looks like a triangle with a line across the middle, like a little roof or a tent.
hints: It's the last letter in your name, LUCA!
It looks like a tent or a mountain with a belt.
I'll point near it—can you touch it?

questions.prompt: Find the letter M

options.feedback: Yes! That's M, and it says /mmm/!

questions.explanation: The letter M has two tall lines with two mountains in the middle. It says /mmm/ like in 'mom' or 'mouse.'
hints: It says /mmm/ like 'mmmmouse.'
It has two humps like mountains.
Let me show you: this one!

questions.prompt: Find the letter B

options.feedback: Excellent! That's B!

questions.explanation: The letter B has one line and two bumps on the side. It says /buh/ like in 'ball' or 'book.'
hints: It says /buh/ like 'ball.'
It has two bumps on one side.
Here, let's look together!

steps.materialsInstructions: Bingo board with letters arranged in 2 rows of 3, or 3 rows of 2—whatever is clearest. Letters should be large (at least 2 inches tall).

steps.title: Letter Bingo Game - Round 2
steps.instructions: Say: 'Let's play again!' Remove the covers. This time, call the letters in a different order. You can add a little challenge: 'Can you find the letter that says /mmm/?' or 'Find the first letter in your name.' Keep it quick and playful. Celebrate again when he gets BINGO.

questions.prompt: Find the first letter in your name

options.feedback: Yes! L is the first letter in LUCA!

questions.explanation: The first letter in LUCA is L.
hints: Your name is LUCA. What letter comes first?
It's the letter L!
Let's say your name slowly: L-U-C-A.

questions.prompt: Find the letter that says /mmm/

options.feedback: Great listening! That's M!

questions.explanation: The letter M makes the /mmm/ sound, like in 'mom' or 'mouse.'
hints: Say /mmm/ like a mouse: mmmmmm.
It's the letter M!
Let me show you!

questions.prompt: Find the letter B

options.feedback: Perfect! That's B!

questions.explanation: The letter B has one line and two bumps. It says /buh/.
hints: It says /buh/ like 'ball.'
It has two bumps.
Right here!

steps.materialsInstructions: Same bingo board and covers.

steps.title: Celebration and Letter Review
steps.instructions: Say: 'You played Letter Bingo two times! Let's name all the letters one more time together.' Point to each letter and say it with Luca. Then give him a high five or let him jump three times to celebrate. End on a happy, confident note: 'You are learning your letters!'
steps.materialsInstructions: Bingo board for final review.

materialsNeeded: Bingo board with 6 letters: L, U, C, A, M, B (large, clear uppercase)
6 small objects to use as covers (blocks, pom-poms, coins, or small toys)
Letter cards matching the bingo letters (optional but helpful)
list.parentInstructions: This is a quick, playful letter recognition game. Keep your voice warm and encouraging. If Luca struggles with a letter, point to it and say the name together. The goal is recognition practice, not perfection. Let him celebrate when he gets BINGO! If he finishes quickly, you can play a bonus round or stop on a high note.

list.theme: Movement and objects (using balls, blocks, and toys Luca loves)
list.title: Two-Step Direction Adventure
list.objective: Follow two-step directions with visual object support to improve auditory comprehension, memory sequencing, and receptive language skills.

steps.title: Movement Warm-Up with Objects
steps.instructions: Place all objects on the floor in front of Luca. Say: 'Let's touch each thing!' Point to each object and have Luca touch it and name it with you: 'ball,' 'basket,' 'red block,' 'blue block,' 'animal,' 'pillow.' Then do 5 jumps together to get ready.
steps.materialsInstructions: Spread materials on floor where Luca can see and reach them easily.

steps.title: Two-Step Direction Practice
steps.instructions: Give Luca two-step directions using the objects. Use AND to connect the steps. Point to objects as you say them. Start with modeling: 'Watch me first.' Then let Luca try. Give 4 different directions from the question list below. Praise immediately after each try: 'You did both steps! Great listening!'

questions.prompt: Get the ball AND put it in the basket.

options.text: Picks up ball and puts it in basket (both steps completed)
options.feedback: Perfect! You did both steps! You got the ball AND put it in the basket!

options.text: Picks up ball only (stops after first step)
options.feedback: Good! You got the ball. Now, put it in the basket. Let's try again together.

options.text: Puts ball somewhere else or drops it
options.feedback: Let's try together. Watch: Get the ball AND put it IN the basket.

questions.correctAnswer: Luca picks up the ball and places it inside the basket
questions.explanation: This two-step direction requires Luca to remember both actions in sequence: retrieve object, then place it in a location.
hints: First, touch the ball. What's next?
Get the ball... now where does it go?
Watch me: I get the ball AND put it in the basket. Now you try.

questions.prompt: Touch the red block AND give it to me.

options.text: Touches red block and gives it to parent (both steps completed)
options.feedback: Excellent listening! You touched the red block AND gave it to me!
Great job touching the red block! Now, give it to me. Let's finish together.

options.text: Touches wrong color or skips touching
options.feedback: Let's find the red block together. This one is red. Touch it AND give it to me.

questions.correctAnswer: Luca touches the red block and hands it to parent
questions.explanation: This direction combines object identification (red block), action (touch), and social action (give to me).
hints: Which one is red? Touch it first.
Good! Now, give it to me.
Watch: I touch the red block AND give it to you. Your turn!

questions.prompt: Pick up the animal AND put it on the pillow.

options.text: Picks up animal and places it on pillow (both steps completed)
options.feedback: Wonderful! You picked up the animal AND put it on the pillow!

options.text: Picks up animal but doesn't place it on pillow
options.feedback: Good start! You have the animal. Now put it ON the pillow.

options.text: Picks up wrong object or places animal elsewhere
options.feedback: Let's do it together. Pick up the animal AND put it on the pillow.

questions.correctAnswer: Luca picks up the toy animal and places it on the pillow
questions.explanation: Two-step direction with object manipulation and spatial placement.
hints: First, get the animal. What do we do next?
Pick up the animal... now where does it go?
Let me show you: Pick up the animal AND put it on the pillow.

questions.prompt: Touch the blue block AND put it in the basket.

options.text: Touches blue block and puts it in basket (both steps completed)
options.feedback: Amazing listening! You touched the blue block AND put it in the basket!
You touched the blue block! Now, put it in the basket. Let's finish.

options.text: Touches wrong color or places block elsewhere
options.feedback: Let's find the blue block. Here it is! Touch it AND put it in the basket.

questions.correctAnswer: Luca touches the blue block and places it in the basket
questions.explanation: Combines color identification, action, and spatial placement in two sequential steps.
hints: Which block is blue? Touch it first.
Great! Now, where does it go? In the basket!
Watch me: Touch the blue block AND put it in the basket.

steps.materialsInstructions: Keep objects visible. Point and use gestures. Model first if Luca hesitates.

steps.title: Celebration and Review
steps.instructions: Say: 'You followed directions with TWO steps! That's hard work!' High-five or fist bump. Ask: 'Which one was your favorite?' Let Luca point or say. End with: 'You're a great listener!' Put materials away together using one-step directions: 'Put the ball away,' 'Put the blocks away.'
steps.materialsInstructions: Use cleanup as bonus one-step direction practice.

materialsNeeded: 1 ball (any size)
1 basket or large bowl
4-5 colorful blocks (red, blue, yellow, green)
1 toy animal or car
1 small pillow or beanbag
list.parentInstructions: This is a speech-language activity focused on following two-step directions. Your role is to give clear, short directions using objects Luca can see and touch. Use gestures and pointing to support understanding. Praise effort immediately. If Luca struggles, model the action first, then do it together, then let him try alone. Keep your tone playful and supportive. Stop after 6-7 minutes or sooner if frustration appears.

list.theme: Building and creating with blocks (connects to Luca's love of Legos and building)
list.title: Block Pattern Building Game
list.objective: Luca will recognize, continue, and create simple AB patterns using colored blocks with hands-on practice.

steps.title: Pattern Introduction
steps.instructions: Parent: Place blocks in front of Luca. Say: 'We're going to make patterns with blocks! Watch me.' Build a simple red-blue-red-blue pattern (4 blocks total). Point to each block as you say the colors aloud slowly: 'Red, blue, red, blue.' Then ask: 'What comes next?' Point to the next empty space.

questions.prompt: Point to the pattern red-blue-red-blue. What color block comes next?

options.feedback: Yes! Blue comes next. The pattern is red-blue-red-blue!
Let's look again. The pattern is red-blue-red-blue. After red comes blue!

questions.explanation: The pattern is red-blue repeating, so after red comes blue.
hints: Look at the pattern. It goes red, blue, red, blue. What came after red last time?
Point to the first blue block. This came after red. What should come after this red?
Let's say it together: red, blue, red... what's next?

steps.materialsInstructions: Use 4 blocks (2 red, 2 blue) to create the model pattern. Keep extra blocks nearby.

steps.title: Continue the Pattern Together
steps.instructions: Parent: Hand Luca a blue block and guide him to place it in the pattern. Say: 'Yes! Blue! You continued the pattern!' Now say: 'Let's keep going. What comes next?' Point to the next space. If Luca hesitates, point to the beginning of the pattern and say the colors aloud. Let Luca place the next block (red). Do 2-3 more blocks together, continuing the red-blue pattern.

questions.prompt: You just put down blue. What color comes next in our red-blue pattern?

options.feedback: Perfect! Red comes after blue in this pattern!
Let's look at the pattern again. After blue comes red. Red-blue-red-blue!

questions.explanation: After blue in the red-blue pattern, red comes next.
hints: Look at the beginning. After blue, what color came?
The pattern is red-blue-red-blue. After blue comes...
Let's point to each one: red, blue, red, blue, red, blue... see the pattern?

questions.prompt: Now you put down red. What comes after red?

options.feedback: Excellent! You're building the pattern perfectly!
Let's check the pattern. After red comes blue!

questions.explanation: After red comes blue in this repeating pattern.
hints: Look at what happened before. After red came what color?
Let's say the pattern: red, blue, red, blue... what's next?
Point to the pattern and count: red (1), blue (2), red (3), blue (4), red (5)... what's 6?

steps.materialsInstructions: Hand Luca the correct color block if he's unsure. Have extra blocks ready.

steps.title: New Pattern Challenge
steps.instructions: Parent: Clear the blocks. Say: 'Great job! Now let's try a new pattern!' Build a blue-blue-red pattern (3 blocks). Say the colors aloud as you place them: 'Blue, blue, red.' Then ask: 'What comes next?' Let Luca continue the pattern for 3-4 more blocks. If Luca struggles, say the pattern aloud together while pointing. Celebrate each correct placement!

questions.prompt: The new pattern is blue-blue-red. What color comes next?

options.feedback: Yes! The pattern starts over with blue!
Let's look at the beginning. Blue-blue-red, then blue-blue-red again. After red comes blue!

questions.explanation: The pattern is blue-blue-red repeating, so it starts again with blue.
hints: The pattern is blue, blue, red, then it starts over. What was the first color?
Let's say it: blue, blue, red, blue, blue, red... what comes after red?
After the pattern finishes with red, it starts again. The pattern starts with...

questions.prompt: You just put down blue. In the blue-blue-red pattern, what comes next?

options.feedback: Perfect! Two blues together, then red!
Not yet! We need two blues first. Blue, blue, THEN red!

questions.explanation: The pattern has two blues before red, so after the first blue comes another blue.
hints: Look at the beginning. There are two blues together. Blue, blue, then red.
Count the blues at the start: one blue, two blues, then red. You just put down one blue...
The pattern goes blue, blue, red. You need two blues before the red!

steps.materialsInstructions: Create a new pattern: blue-blue-red. Have blocks ready for Luca to continue.

materialsNeeded: Blocks in 2 different colors (red and blue work well, or any 2 colors available)
Flat surface like table or floor
6-10 blocks of each color (12-20 blocks total)
list.parentInstructions: This is a short, playful pattern activity. Your role is to MODEL first, then let Luca BUILD and CONTINUE patterns. Keep it fast-paced and fun. If Luca gets frustrated, do it together hand-over-hand. Celebrate effort! This builds early math reasoning and visual sequencing. Stop at 6 minutes even if not finished—short and successful is better than long and frustrating.
list.onStruggleStrategy: scaffold - If Luca struggles, parent should immediately model by pointing to the pattern and saying colors aloud slowly. Do the next step hand-over-hand together. Reduce to the simpler red-blue-red-blue pattern. Offer only 2 block choices (correct color and one other). Celebrate any attempt. If frustration appears, stop and say 'You worked hard! That's good learning!' and end the activity positively.

list.theme: toy animals and imaginative play
list.title: Animal Adventure Story
list.objective: Practice organizing and telling a simple 3-part story using sequencing words (first, next, last) with concrete toy support.

steps.title: Animal Movement Warm-Up
steps.instructions: Parent says: 'Before we tell stories, let's BE the animals!' Pick an animal and move like it for 30 seconds. Examples: hop like a bunny, stomp like an elephant, crawl like a bear, slither like a snake. Let Luca choose the animal and lead the movement. Parent copies him. This regulates attention and connects body to the story activity.
steps.materialsInstructions: No materials needed—just space to move.

steps.title: Parent Models the 3-Part Story
steps.instructions: Parent picks 3 toy animals and places them in front of Luca. Parent says: 'I'm going to tell a story with these animals. Watch and listen.' Parent physically moves each animal as they tell the story using this structure: 'FIRST, the [animal] came to the park. NEXT, the [animal] played on the slide. LAST, the [animal] went home.' Use exaggerated gestures. Move the animal to a 'home' spot (box or basket) at the end. Keep it simple and visual. Ask Luca: 'What happened first?' Point to the first animal if needed.

questions.prompt: What happened FIRST in my story?

options.text: The animal came to the park
options.feedback: Yes! First, the animal came to the park. You remembered!
That happened last. What happened first? Let's look at the first animal.
That happened next. What happened before that? Let's try again.

questions.correctAnswer: The [animal name] came to the park
questions.explanation: This question checks whether Luca understood the sequence. Accept any reasonable answer that identifies the first event, even if wording is incomplete.
hints: Point to the first animal and say: 'This one did something first. What did it do?'
Say: 'The [animal] came to...' and wait for Luca to finish or repeat.
Say the answer together: 'FIRST, the [animal] came to the park.' Then ask Luca to repeat it.

steps.materialsInstructions: Use 3 toy animals and the box/basket for 'home.'

steps.title: Luca Creates His Own Story
steps.instructions: Now it's Luca's turn! Parent says: 'Now YOU tell a story with these animals.' Let Luca choose 3 animals. Parent provides structure with sentence starters if needed: 'FIRST, the [animal]...' Encourage Luca to physically move each animal as he talks. If he struggles, model the first part together, then let him try the next part. If he rushes or skips parts, gently prompt: 'What happens next?' Point to the animal. If he uses incomplete sentences, expand them without correcting: 'Yes, the lion came to the playground!' Accept approximations—this is about organizing the sequence, not perfect grammar.

questions.prompt: Tell me your story. What happened FIRST?
questions.correctAnswer: Any answer describing the first animal's action using 'first' or indicating initial event (e.g., 'First the bear came' or 'The dog walked in')
questions.explanation: This is open-ended narrative practice. The goal is for Luca to produce a sentence about the first event, with or without scaffolding.
hints: Start him off: 'FIRST, the [animal he chose]...' and wait.
Point to the first animal and say: 'What did this one do first?'
Say it together: 'FIRST, the [animal] came to [place].' Then have him repeat it alone.
Move the animal for him and say: 'Tell me what I'm doing: FIRST, the...'

questions.prompt: What happened NEXT?
questions.correctAnswer: Any answer describing the second animal's action using 'next' or indicating middle event (e.g., 'Next the bear played' or 'He jumped')
questions.explanation: This prompt scaffolds the middle of the story. Accept any reasonable middle action, even if brief.
hints: Say: 'NEXT, the [animal]...' and wait.
Point to the second animal and say: 'What did this one do?'
Offer two choices: 'Did the animal play or sleep?'
Say it together, then have him repeat alone.

questions.prompt: What happened LAST?
questions.correctAnswer: Any answer describing the final animal's action using 'last' or indicating ending event (e.g., 'Last the bear went home' or 'He left')
questions.explanation: This completes the 3-part structure. The goal is closure with the word 'last' or a clear ending action.
hints: Say: 'LAST, the [animal]...' and wait.
Point to the 'home' box and say: 'Where did the animal go last?'
Say it together: 'LAST, the [animal] went home.'
Move the animal to the box and say: 'Tell me: LAST, the...'

steps.materialsInstructions: Luca uses 3 toy animals of his choice and the box/basket for 'home.'

steps.title: Celebration and Replay
steps.instructions: Parent says: 'You told a whole story! Let's tell it one more time together!' Parent and Luca physically move the animals and say the story together using the structure: 'FIRST... NEXT... LAST...' This reinforces the sequence and ends with success. Give specific praise: 'You used first, next, and last! You made a story with a beginning, a middle, and an end. Great storytelling!' High five or animal sound celebration.
steps.materialsInstructions: Same toy animals and box.

materialsNeeded: 3 toy animals (child's choice)
small box or basket for 'home'
optional: small blanket or mat for 'play area'
list.parentInstructions: This activity builds narrative language skills, which is a priority speech goal for Luca. Your role is to scaffold the story by modeling the structure first, then helping Luca create his own version. Use short sentences, visual cues (the toys themselves), and the sequencing words 'first,' 'next,' and 'last.' If Luca struggles, offer sentence starters like 'First, the ___ came to...' Celebrate effort, not perfection. Keep the story simple and concrete. If he rushes or gets distracted, gently redirect: 'Show me with the animal.' This is practice for organizing language, not testing.
list.onStruggleStrategy: If Luca struggles with any question, immediately use the hints in order. If he still cannot respond, model the answer, say it together, and move on. Do NOT drill or repeat more than 3 times. Keep the tone playful. If frustration appears, pause, offer a movement break (animal walk for 20 seconds), then return or end positively. The goal is exposure to narrative structure, not mastery in one session. Celebrate effort: 'You worked hard telling a story. That's real learning!'

list.theme: Detective game using favorite toys and household objects
list.title: I Spy Speech Game: Beginning Sounds
list.objective: Student will identify and name objects that begin with target sounds /b/, /m/, /s/, and /p/, improving phonological awareness, speech articulation, and turn-taking skills.

steps.title: Sound Detective Warm-Up
steps.instructions: Say: 'We're going to be sound detectives! Let's warm up our listening ears and our mouths.' Practice making the target sounds together with exaggerated mouth movements: /b/ /b/ /b/ (lips pop), /m/ /m/ /m/ (lips closed, hum), /s/ /s/ /s/ (teeth together, snake sound), /p/ /p/ /p/ (puff of air). Do 3 big jumps after each sound to 'wake up our detective brains.'
steps.materialsInstructions: None needed—just space to jump

steps.title: I Spy with /b/
steps.instructions: Parent models first: 'I spy with my little eye something that starts with /b/.' Emphasize the /b/ sound. Point to or look at the object (ball, book, or banana). Wait for Luca to guess. If he guesses correctly, celebrate! If he needs help, give two choices: 'Is it the ball or the marker?' After parent's turn, Luca gets a turn to spy something with /b/. Accept any attempt and model the correct sound if needed.

questions.prompt: I spy with my little eye something that starts with /b/. What is it?

options.feedback: Yes! Ball starts with /b/! /b/ /b/ ball!
Marker starts with /m/, not /b/. Let's try again!

questions.explanation: The ball starts with the /b/ sound. We hear /b/ at the beginning.
hints: Look around—do you see something round?
It's something you can throw and catch.
Point to two objects: 'Is it the ball or the marker?'

questions.prompt: Now you spy something that starts with /b/!

options.feedback: Great spying! Book starts with /b/!
That starts with /s/. Let's find a /b/ word.

questions.explanation: Book starts with /b/. Good listening and good turn-taking!
hints: Look for something we read.
It has pages.
You can point and parent will guess!

steps.materialsInstructions: Have 3-4 objects starting with /b/ visible in the area

steps.title: I Spy with /m/ and /s/
steps.instructions: Continue the game with two more sounds. Parent models: 'I spy with my little eye something that starts with /m/.' (marker, mouse, or milk). After Luca guesses, he takes a turn. Then repeat with /s/ sound (sock, spoon, star). Keep turns quick. If Luca struggles, reduce to two choices and point. Praise all attempts: 'You're listening so well!' or 'Good try—let's say it together.'

questions.prompt: I spy with my little eye something that starts with /m/. What is it?

options.feedback: Yes! /m/ /m/ marker! Perfect!
Sock starts with /s/. Let's find the /m/ sound.

questions.explanation: Marker starts with /m/. Great job hearing that sound!
hints: It's something we use to draw or write.
It has a cap on top.
Is it the marker or the sock?

questions.prompt: I spy with my little eye something that starts with /s/. What is it?

options.feedback: Perfect! /s/ /s/ sock! You're a great detective!
Pillow starts with /p/. Let's listen for /s/.

questions.explanation: Sock starts with /s/, the snake sound!
hints: Make the snake sound: /s/ /s/ /s/.
It goes on your foot.
Is it the sock or the pillow?

questions.prompt: Your turn to spy something with /s/!

options.feedback: Wonderful! /s/ /s/ spoon!
Bear starts with /b/. Let's find /s/.

questions.explanation: Spoon starts with /s/. Great turn-taking!
hints: Look for something in the kitchen.
We use it to eat.
Point to it and parent will guess!

steps.materialsInstructions: Have 2-3 objects for /m/ and 2-3 objects for /s/ visible

steps.title: I Spy with /p/ and Celebration
steps.instructions: Final round with /p/ sound. Parent: 'I spy with my little eye something that starts with /p/.' (pillow, pen, puzzle piece). Luca guesses and takes his turn. End with celebration: 'You were such a good sound detective today! Let's give ourselves 5 claps!' Clap together. If time allows, let Luca choose his favorite sound to spy one more time.

questions.prompt: I spy with my little eye something that starts with /p/. What is it?

options.feedback: Amazing! /p/ /p/ pillow! You're a sound superstar!
Banana starts with /b/. Let's find /p/.

questions.explanation: Pillow starts with /p/. You did it!
hints: It's soft.
We put our head on it when we sleep.
Is it the pillow or the banana?

steps.materialsInstructions: Have 2-3 objects for /p/ visible

materialsNeeded: Household objects or toys beginning with /b/: ball, book, banana, block, bear, bottle
Objects beginning with /m/: marker, mouse, milk, mirror
Objects beginning with /s/: sock, spoon, star, stick
Objects beginning with /p/: pillow, pen, puzzle piece, picture
list.parentInstructions: This is a playful speech game that builds sound awareness for reading readiness. Take turns being the 'spy.' Use exaggerated mouth movements when saying sounds. Keep it playful—if Luca needs help, give a visual hint by pointing to two choices. Praise effort and attempts, not just correct answers. Keep the pace quick and fun. If he loses focus, do a quick movement break (5 jumping jacks) and return.
list.onStruggleStrategy: If Luca struggles, reduce to two visual choices and point. Model the sound with exaggerated mouth movements. Accept approximations and model correct production. If frustration appears, pause for 5 jumping jacks and return. Always end with an easy success round.
